Facts.text: Yazdegerd III begins his reign as the last King of Kings of the Iranian Sasanian Empire
Virginia becomes an English crown colony following the bankruptcy of the London Company
British fleet occupies Cape Breton on St Lawrence River
Battle at Piacenza: Austria & Sardinia beat Spanish and French army
British capture strategic Fort Beauséjour, eastern Canada, expelling the Acadians and renaming it Fort Cumberland
Formation of Harrodsburg, Kentucky
Holland forbids the wearing of orange clothes
1st stone laid at biggest Dutch grain windmill De Walvisch in Schiedam
The world's first book club, The Roxburghe Club, is founded before the infamous sale of the Duke of Roxburghe's library at a dinner hosted by the 2nd Earl of Spencer at St Albans Tavern, London
Denmark Vesey (aka Telemaque) Black American carpenter accused of planning a slave rebellion in South Carolina; tried and convicted, later executed by hanging
Dutch King William I opens the Fijenoord shipyard at Rotterdam
Battle of Kellogg's Grove, Illinois - Illinois militia fight small force of Native Americans
London Working Men's Association forms
Battle of Vienna, Virginia and Secessionville, South Carolina (James Island)
Battle of Lynchburg, Virginia; Confederate troops fend off forces of Union general David Hunter
Skirmish at Golgotha, Georgia
Ancient Arabic Order of Nobles of Mystic Shrine founded, NYC
The University Tests Act allows students to enter the Universities of Oxford, Cambridge and Durham without religious tests, except for courses in theology
Austria-Hungary and Serbia sign military treaty
17" hailstones weighing 1.75 lbs fall in Dubuque Iowa
1st baseball "Ladies' Day" - NY Gothams beat Cleveland Blues 5-2 at the Polo Grounds in NYC
Victoria Hall disaster: The distribution of free toys in Sunderland, England causes a crowd crush and the deaths of 183 children
America's first purpose-built roller coaster ride, the Switchback Railway, opens at Coney Island, New York, built by LaMarcus Thompson
German-American F.W. Rueckheim introduces "Cracker Jack" brand snack food consisting of caramel-coated popcorn and peanuts
Temperature hits 127°F at Fort Mojave, California
A treaty annexing the Republic of Hawaii to the United States is signed; the Republic would not be dissolved until a year later
China's emperor De Zong (Guang Xu) receives Kang Youwei
In China, a fire is set by Boxers, virtually destroying the Western Quarter and spreading to engulf many Chinese landmarks
"The Wizard of Oz" musical first opens in Chicago, Illinois
1st Highlander (Yankee) shut-out victory 1-0 over White Sox
Pepsi-Cola is trademarked by inventor Caleb Bradham
Reichstag elections with gains for the Social Democratic Party of Germany
Eugen Schauman assassinates Nikolai Bobrikov, Governor-General of Finland
First US airplane is sold commercially by Glenn Curtiss for $5,000
A 772-gram stony meteorite strikes Earth near Kilbourn, Columbia County, Wisconsin, damaging a barn
South African Government pass the segregationist Native Land Act, which restricts purchase or lease of land by native Africans
The foundation of the British Women's Institute
MLB Boston Brave's pitcher Tom Hughes no-hitter beats Pittsburgh, 2-0; at the time it is his 2nd career no-hitter, but a 1991 rule change wiped his 1910 effort
Passing of the Military Services Bill introduces conscription in New Zealand
1st All Russian Congress of the Soviets convenes in Petrograd, Russia [OS Jun 3]
Henry Berliner demonstrates his helicopter to US Bureau of Aeronautics
Irish republicans are beaten in a national election; the vote is in favor of the Treaty of London, which leaves the Irish Free State as a dominion within the British Commonwealth
South Africa all out 30 v England in 48 minutes, Gilligan 6-7
The most famous Young Pioneer camp of the USSR, Artek, is established
The Union Government rejects a round-table conference with India on the grounds that it will constitute interference in South African affairs
Otto E. Funk, 62, ends marathon walk (NY to SF, 4165 miles in 183 days)
Sovnarkom establishes decree time in the USSR
Austrian government of Ender falls
Germany forbids SA/SS-gang fights
Sutcliffe and Holmes make 555 opening cricket stand for Yorkshire v Essex
US Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) created
US National Industrial Recovery Act becomes law (later struck down)
Communist government installed in Lithuania
General De Gaulle arrives in Bordeaux
1st US federally owned airport opened Washington, D.C.
Iceland adopts constitution
US bombs Kyushu, Japan
Boo Ferriss loses to Yanks 3-2 after starting his career with 8 wins
1st network news-Dumont's "News from Washington"
Pravda denounces Marshall Plan
Gas turbine-electric locomotive demonstrated at Erie, Pennsylvania
Soviet Fighters shoots down Swedish Air Force Catalina as it participates in search for missing Dakota DC-3, crew survives
Despite Johnny Mize 2,000th hit, NY Yankees lose ending an 18 game winning streak and also ending St Louis Brown 14 game losing streak
Dutch military conscription shortened from 20 to 18 months
French offensive in Algeria
White Sox reliever Dixie Howell hits 2 HRs to beat Washington Senators 8-6
"Flip Top Box" by Dicky Doo & The Don'ts hits #46
Government troops land on North-Celebes, Indonesia

South African Apartheid government efforts to remove Black people from Cato Manor close to the Durban city center to newly established black township Kwa Mashu, on outskirts met with violent resistance
PresidentEisenhowercancels trip to Japan
Dave Garroway is fired as host of NBC's "Today Show"
Reconnaissance satellite Discoverer 25 launched from the US
Soviet ballet dancer Rudolf Nureyev defects to West at Le Bourget Airport in Paris, France
2 US army officers killed in Saigon
Quake strikes Niigata, Japan
20th Tony Awards: "Marat/Sade" (play) & "Man of La Mancha" (musical) win
50,000 attend first day of the Monterey International Pop Festival - beginning of the Summer of Love
The first Montreux Jazz Festival is held in Switzerland, created by Claude Nobs

US Supreme Court rules suspension of Adam Clayton Powell, Jr from House of Representatives violated Article I of the Constitution by citing reasons beyond the exclusive list of qualifications in the article
Kenneth A. Gibson elected 1st black mayor of Newark, New Jersey
Race riots in Miami, Florida
Groningen soccer team forms in Groningen, Netherlands
Racial disturbance in Jacksonville, Florida
The largest single-site hydro-electric power project in Canada starts at Churchill Falls, Labrador
Italy's Communist party PCI, wins
Randy Farland finds a 14-leaf clover near Sioux Falls, South Dakota
US Supreme Court rules uniform minimum legal fees are a violation
Soweto Uprising: Over 10,000 students in Soweto, South Africa, march against the use of Afrikaans as a medium of instruction in Black secondary schools; clashes with police kills dozens
"Beatlemania" opens on Broadway
Fianna Fail party wins Irish elections
Ron Guidry's 1st complete game, 7-0 over KC Royals
Cincinnati RedTom Seaverno-hits St Louis Cards, 4-0
Muslim Brotherhood kills 62 sheiks in Aleppo, Syria
Single "Logical Song" by Supertramp peaks at #6
US Supreme Court rules that live, human-made micro-organisms created in labs could be patentable, in Diamond v. Chakrabarty
Britain makes request to Argentina to arrange for return of prisoners
Political party "Official Monster Raving Loony Party" is founded by English rocker Screaming Lord Sutch
Charlos Vieira completes 191 hr "nonstop" cycling in Leiria, Portugal
European Space Agency launches European Comm Satellite 1, Oscar 10
Ringo Starrreleases album "Old Wave" album in West Germany; soon after releases follow in Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, the Netherlands, Mexico, and Brazil
Edwin Moses wins his 100th consecutive 400-meter hurdles race
Matt de Waal finishes 14,290-mi round trip from Salt Lake City (106d)
Willie Banks of USA sets triple jump record (58 feet 11 inches) in Indianapolis
1 day general strike in South Africa
Joe Robbie Stadium in Miami, opens
New York City subway gunman Bernhard Getz acquitted on all but gun possession charges after shooting 4 black youths who tried to rob him
Boston Red Sox Barrett steals home
In Santa Barbara, CA, a team of 32 divers begins cycling underwater on a standard tricycle to complete 116.66 miles in 75 hours and 20 minutes
"Ghostbusters II", American supernatural comedy film sequel, directed by Ivan Reitman, starring Bill Murray andDan Aykroydpremieres
"U Can't Touch This" by MC Hammer peaks at #8
Nelson &Winnie Mandelavisit Leidseplein, Amsterdam
NHL Draft: Cornwall Royals (OHL) right wing Owen Nolan first pick by Quebec Nordiques
-23] NYC Mayor Dinkins declares "Joseph Doherty Week"
Minnesota Twins win a team record 15 games in a row
Otis Nixon steals NL record 6 bases in 1 day
With 3 runs in 9th, Baltimore Orioles end Minnesota Twins 15 game winning streak, with 6-5 win
British postage stamp celebrates 350th anniversary of the Battle of Edgehill
Caspar Weinberger, former US Secretary of Defense (1981-87), indicted on Iran-contra charge
Colorado Rockies 1st minor league team's (Bend Oregon in Class A) game
Longest salami is 68ft-9in long & 25ft in circumference, weighs 1,492lbs/5oz in Flekkefjord, Norway
MLB Boston Red Sox Jeff Reardon sets record of 342 saves (vs Yanks 1-0)
Marlins outfielderAndre Dawsonhits his 400th NL career HR (429)
Salt Lake City awarded the XIX Winter Olympics in 2002
The Dairat Labguer massacre in Algeria; 50 people are killed
Stanley Cup Final, MCI Center, Washington, D.C.: Detroit Red Wings go back-to-back, defeating debutant finalists Washington Capitals, 4-1 for a 4-0 series sweep

Israel complies with UN Security Council Resolution 425 after 22 years, which calls on Israel to completely withdraw from Lebanon. Israel withdraws from all of Lebanon, except the disputed Shebaa Farms.
30 people are killed and 15 injured after a bus falls into a gorge in Osmanabad, India
32 people are killed by a car bomb in Baghdad
Coca-Cola begins business in Myanmar after 60 years
Liu Yang is the first Chinese female astronaut in space aboard the Shenzhou 9 - 49 years to the day since the first woman in space cosmonautValentina Tereshkova
20 people are killed by a series of car bombings across Iraq
British MP (L) Jo Cox is shot and killed outside her constituency surgery in Birstall, West Yorkshire
Philadelphia is the first US city to pass a tax on sweetened drinks
Amazon announces it is buying Whole Foods for $13.7 billion
US PresidentDonald Trumpreinstates Cuban travel and business restrictions after they were loosened by President Obama
Two suicide bombers attack Damboa in Borno state, Nigeria, killing at least 31 people
World Cup: Video Assistant Referee (VAR) technology is used for the first time, awarding France a penalty in 2-1 win over Australia in Kazan
India imposes retaliatory tariffs on 28 US goods
Massive unprecedented power cut affects all of Argentina, Uruguay and parts of Paraguay
Three suicide bomb attacks kill 30 people in Konduga, north east Nigeria
At least 20 Indian soldiers killed in 1st deadly clash on the Chinese Indian border in 45 years in the Galwan Valley, Himalayas
Ninety-year-old US cosmetics company Revlon files for bankruptcy, blaming supply issues and rising costs
Attack on Mpondwe Lhubiriha Secondary School in Mpondwe, Uganda by Islamic State-linked militants leaves about 40 pupils dead
Scientific teams in the UK and Israel claim to have grown synthetic human embryos equivalent to that of 14-day-old natural embryos sparking controversy
The Recording Academy announces new rules for Grammy Awards aimed at AI use - stating “only human creators” can win but a work containing AI eligible as long as human input is meaningful
US truck driver Robert Bowers found guilty of the deadliest attack on Jews in the US, of shooting 11 worshippers at the Tree of Life synagogue, Pittsburgh, in 2018
105 year-old Virginia Hislop graduates with a masters diploma in education from Stanford University, 83 years after she had to leave early
51st G7 summit begins in Kananaskis, Canada, as the first summit for many recently elected world leaders
Battle of Stoke Field, Nottinghamshire: English Tudor KingHenry VIIdefeats the remaining Yorkists led by John de la Pole and Lord Lovell in the last battle of the Wars of the Roses
Mary, Queen of Scots, imprisoned in Lochleven Castle, Scotland
US GeneralAnthony Waynecaptures Stony Point, New York, inflicting heavy losses on the British
Battle at Quatre-Bras:Duke of Wellington's English forces engage the French in a preliminary battle two days before the Battle of Waterloo
French army underNapoleondefeats Prussia in the Battle of Ligny, Napoleon's last military victory
Abraham Lincolnsays "A house divided against itself cannot stand" accepting Illinois Republican Party's nomination for the Senate
US President Ulysses Grant decrees a portion of Wallowa Valley, Oregon for the Native American tribeNez Perce. Order is rescinded two years later and the tribe is forcibly re-located to Oklahoma.
W. S. GilbertandArthur Sullivan's comic opera "HMS Pinafore" debuts at Bowery Theater NYC
John Abbottbecomes Canada's third prime minister
Ford Motors underHenry Fordincorporates
Roald Amundsencommences the first east-west navigation of the Northwest Passage by leaving Oslo, Norway
Bloomsday, the date of the fictional events inJames Joyce's novel "Ulysses"

TsarNicolas IIof Russia dissolves the Second Duma (parliament) and issues an edict that will increase representation of propertied classes while reducing that of peasants, workers and national minorities
The Republican Party convenes in Chicago where PresidentTheodore RooseveltpicksWilliam Howard Taftas his successor
Jim Thorpemakes his pro baseball pitching debut for Rocky Mount (ECL) with a 4-2 win, causing him to forfeit his Olympic gold medals
US PresidentHerbert Hooverand Vice PresidentCharles Curtisrenominated by Republican Convention
DutchQueen Wilhelminaopens the Waal bridge with the longest arch in Europe at the time
PopePius XIreceives Anton Mussert (Dutch Nazi collaborator)
Boston first basemanJimmie Foxxis walked a record 6 consecutive times by St Louis Browns; Red Sox still win, 12-8
KingGeorge VIvisits General Montgomery's HQ in Normandy
Ngô Đình Diệmelected Prime Minister of the State of Vietnam (South Vietnam)
PopePius XIIex-communicatesArgentine President JuanPerón
"Psycho", a psychological horror film directed byAlfred Hitchcockand starringJanet Leigh,Anthony Perkins, andVera Miles, opens in New York City
Soviet space mission Vostok 6 is launched withValentina Tereshkovaonboard, who becomes the first woman in space
Rowan & Martin host "TheDean MartinShow" Summer Series, on NBC-TV
RCA releasesDavid Bowie's fifth studio album "The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars,"; considered his artistic breakthrough
NBA Milwaukee Bucks tradeKareem Abdul-Jabbarand Walt Wesley to L.A. Lakers for 4 players
Leonid Brezhnevnamed Chairman of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union
Oracle Corporation is incorporated in Redwood Shores, California, as Software Development Laboratories (SDL) byLarry Ellison, Bob Miner and Ed Oates
Film "Grease" opens, starringJohn TravoltaandOlivia Newton-John, based on the 1971 Broadway musical
Ringo Starrreleases album "Bad Boy"
Boston Red SoxCarl Yastrzemskihits his 1,000th extra base hit
Musical comedy film "The Blues Brothers", starringDan AykroydandJohn Belushi, and directed by John Landis, premieres in Chicago, Illinois
Columbia Records releases "Somewhere in the Stars", the fourth studio album byRosanne Cash; produced by husband Rodney Crowell, and featuring her fatherJohnny Cashon one track
General SecretaryYuri Andropovappointed President of the Soviet Union
PopeJohn Paul IIvisits Poland
Funeral forImre Nagy, leader of Hungarian uprising in 1956
Ken Griffey Jr.slugs his 100th career home run in Seattle
Canadian goaltenderMartin Brodeurbecomes 1st New Jersey Devil to win Calder Trophy, for Rookie of the Year
"Batman Forever" opens with a record $528 million weekend starringVal Kilmer,Chris O'Donnell,Tommy Lee JonesandNicole Kidman
Thabo Mbekiis elected 2nd President of a democratic South Africa
"Hot In Cleveland", cable channel TV Land's first original sitcom premieres; starringValerie Bertinelli, Jane Leeves, Wendie Malick, andBetty White
Hockey HOF right wingCam Neelyis named President of his former team, the Boston Bruins
Collapse of a stage at aRadioheadconcert kills one person in Toronto, Ontario
TV personality and real estate mogulDonald Trumplaunches his campaign for the Republican nomination for US President at Trump Tower
BeyoncéandJay-Zrelease their joint album "Everything is Love"
Sara Netanyahu, wife of Israeli Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahuadmits to misuse of state funds in court
Support from Manchester United playerMarcus Rashfordand others forces UK Government to make U-turn on summer school meal vouchers
Summit between US PresidentJoe Bidenand Russian PresidentVladimir Putinin Geneva, Switzerland, over issues including cyberattacks and human rights
American rockerBruce Springsteenjoins British legendPaul McCartneyfor two songs (Glory Days; I Wanna Be Your Man) at Met Life Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey
77th Tony Awards: "The Outsiders" best new musical, "Stereophonic" best new play, Jeremy Strong,Sarah Paulson,Daniel Radcliffe, Kara Young acting awards
Spain declares war on Great Britain in support of France and the USA, starting the Great Siege of Gibraltar, which lasts 3 years, 7 months, and 2 weeks
George Stinney, a 14-year-old African-American boy, is wrongfully executed for the murder of two white girls, becoming the youngest person ever executed in 20th-century America
Konoe, 76th Emperor of Japan (1142-55), born in Japan (d. 1155)
Isabella de Coucy, English princess, daughter of Edward III, born at Woodstock Palace (d. 1382)
John Cheke, English classical scholar, statesman, and tutor to King Edward VI, born in Cambridge, England (d. 1557)
Axel Oxenstierna, Swedish statesman (Lord High Chancellor and regent of Sweden), born in Fånö, Sweden (d. 1654)
Joseph Solomon Delmedigo, Italian physician, mathematician, and music theorist, born in Candia, Crete (d. 1655)
Arthur Chichester, 1st Earl of Donegall, Anglo-Irish military soldier and politician, born in Ireland (d. 1675)
Jean de Thévenot, French linguist, traveler and scientist (Voyages), born in Paris, France (d. 1667)[1]
Nathaniel Schnittelbach, German composer and violinist, born in Gdansk, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1667)
Giovanni Paulo Colonna, Italian composer and organ builder, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1695)
Henrietta Anne Stuart, Princess of England and Duchess of Orléans, born in Exeter, England (d. 1670)
Meshech Weare, American farmer, lawyer, and Governor of New Hampshire, born in Third Parish, British America (d. 1786)
Mary Katharine Goddard, American printer and publisher (The Providence Gazette, Maryland Journal), born in New London, Connecticut (d. 1816)[1]
Meingosus Gaelle, German theologian and church composer, born in Buch, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1816)
Salawat Yulayev, Bashkir poet and national hero who led the Pugachev's Rebellion, born in Tekeyevo, Russian Empire (d. 1800)
Francis Johnson, American keyed Kent bugle virtuoso, violinist, bandleader, and composer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1844)[1]
John Linnell, English artist and engraver, born in London (d. 1882)
Julius Plücker, German mathematician and physicist (analytical geometry), born in Elberfeld, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1868)
Edward Davy, English-Australian physician, chemist, and inventor (electric relay), born in Ottery St Mary, England (d. 1885)
Otto Jahn, German archeologist, philologist, and musicologist (W. A. Mozart; critical editions of Persius, Juvenal, and Cicero's Brutus and Orator), born in Kiel, Kingdom of Denmark (d. 1869)[1]
Athanase Coquerel, French Protestant theologian and founder of the Protestant Liberal Union (Jean Calas et sa Famille / Jean Calas and his Family), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1875)[1]
Old Tom Morris, Scottish golfer (British Open 1861-62, 64, 67), born in St. Andrews, Fife, Scotland (d. 1908)
Constantin von Ettingshausen, Austrian geologist and paleobotanist (Contributions to the Tertiary Flora of Australia), born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1897)[1]
Wesley Merritt, American Major General (Union Army), 1st American Military Governor of the Philippines, born in New York City (d. 1910)
Eli Long, American Bvt Major General (Union Army), born in Woodford County, Kentucky (d. 1903)
Cushman K. Davis, American politician, seventh Governor of Minnesota and Senator, born in Henderson, New York (d. 1900)
Ernst Otto Schlick, German naval engineer and inventor of the gyroscope to reduce the rolling of ships, born in Grimma, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1913)[1][2]
David Herold, American pharmacist's assistant who was part of the conspiracy to assassinate Abraham Lincoln, born in Maryland (d. 1865)
David Popper, Bohemian composer and cellist, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1913)
Jan Malát, Czech composer, pedagogue, and musicologist, born in Starý Bydžov, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1915)
Emil Sjogren, Swedish composer and organist, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1918)
Gustav V, king of Sweden (1907-50), born in Stockholm (d. 1950)
Scato Gocko de Vries, Dutch paleographer and librarian known for his Latin manuscript studies (Epistula Sapphus ad Phaonem, Breviarium Grimani), born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1937)[1]
Paul Antonin Vidal, French composer, born in Toulouse, France (d. 1931)
Antonina Nezhdanova, Russian soprano (Rigoletto), born in Kryva Balka, Russian Empire (d. 1950)
Mikhail Vladimirovich Ivanov-Boretsky, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1936)
Fritz Schulz, German law historian (Classical Roman Law), born in Bunzlau, Lower Silesia, German Empire (d. 1957)
Theo Thijssen, Dutch writer (Kees the boy), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Josef Christiaens, Belgian auto racer (first foreigner to enter Indianapolis Motor Speedway "Harvest Classic"), born in Saint-Josse-ten-Noode, Belgium (d. 1919)
Tom Howard, Irish-American vaudeville and radio comedian (It Pays To Be Ignorant), born in County Tyrone, Ireland (d. 1955)
Bobby Clark, American vaudevillian, clown and actor (in comedy act with Paul McCullough), born in Springfield, Ohio (d. 1960)
Peter Stoner, American mathematician, astronomer, and Christian apologist (Science Speaks), born in Douglas, Massachusetts (d. 1980)[1][2]
Lupino Lane [Henry Lupino], British stage, silent and sound screen actor (Love Parade; Me And My Gal: The Deputy Drummer), born in Hackney, London, England (d. 1959)
Norman Kerry [Kaiser], American silent and sound screen actor (A Little Princess; The Phantom of the Opera; Air Eagles), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1956)
Cota Ramaswami, Indian cricket batsman (2 Tests, 1 x 50; Madras) and tennis player (Davis Cup 1922), born in Chennai, India (d. presumed 1990)
Jean-Pierre Peugeot III, French industrialist and leader of the Peugeot company (1928-66), born in Valentigney, France (d. 1966)[1][2]
Murray Leinster [William Fitzgerald Jenkins], American science-fiction author (Time Tunnel; Land of the Giants), born in Norfolk, Virginia (d. 1975)
Elaine Hammerstein, American actress (The Midnight Express), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1948)
Georg Wittig, German chemist and winner of the 1979 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1987)[1]
Helen Traubel, American soprano, born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1972)
Nelson Doubleday, American publisher (Doubleday), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1949)
Jan van Tilburg, Dutch politician and Governor of Suriname (PVDA, 1955-62), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Arthur Pierson, Norwegian-American actor and director (Hat Check Girl), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1975)
Conrad Beck, Swiss composer, born in Lohn, Schaffhausen, Switzerland (d. 1989)
Barbara McClintock, American cytogeneticist (1983 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovery of genetic transposition), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1992)
George Gaylord Simpson, American paleontologist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1984)
Huldreich Georg Früh, Swiss composer (Mein Traum), born in Ganterschwil, Switzerland (d. 1945)
Gerard Holt, Dutch architect (municipal theatres Tilburg and Nijmegen, St. Joseph's Church), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1988)[1]
Alan Fairfax, Australian cricket all-rounder (10 Tests, 4 x 50, 21 wickets, BB 4/31; NSWCA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1955)
Jack Albertson, American actor (Chico and the Man, Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory), born in Malden, Massachusetts (d. 1981)
John Hadfield, British author and publisher (Love on a Branch Line), born in Birmingham, England (d. 1999)
Alfredo Boulton, Venezuelan art critic, photographer, and art historian, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 1995)[1]
Archie Carr, American ecologist and zoologist (conservation of sea turtles), born in Mobile, Alabama (d. 1987)
Willi Boskovsky,  Austrian violinist and conductor (Vienna New Year's Concert, 1955-79), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1991)
Ilona Massey [Hajmássy], Hungarian-American film, stage, and radio actress and singer (Ilona Massey Show), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1974)
Richard Barrer, New Zealand-born chemist (father of zeolite chemistry), born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 1996)
Gilbert Gadoffre, French historian, professor of French and wartime resister, born in Paris (d. 1995)
John Peel, British politician and Member of Parliament for Leicester South East (1957-74), born in England (d. 2004)
Billy Griffith, English cricket wicketkeeper (3 Tests, 1 x 100, HS 140, 5 dismissals; Cambridge Uni CC, Surrey CCC, MCC, Sussex CCC) and administrator (Secretary MCC), born in London (d. 1993)
John Tukey, American mathematician and statistician (Cooley–Tukey algorithm, Fast Fourier Transform), born in New Bedford, Massachusets (d. 2000)[1]
Joseph Kagan, Lithuanian-British businessman, and inventor (Gannex raincoats), born in Kaunas, Russsian Empire (now Lithuania) (d. 1995)
Mariano Rumor, Italian statesman (Prime Minister of Italy 1968-70, 1973-4, born in Vicenza, Italy (d. 1990)
Angelo "Hank" Luisetti, American collegiate basketball forward (Stanford, 1935-38), 1st to use a one-handed shot, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2002)
Francis Lopez, Basque-French chanson and operetta composer (La Belle de Cadiz; Andalousie; The Three Musketeers), born in Montbeliard, France (d. 1995)
Aurelio Lampredi, Italian automobile and aircraft engine designer (Ferrari), born in Livorno, Italy (d. 1989)
Irving Penn, American fashion and fine art photographer, born in Plainfield, New Jersey (d. 2009)
Bob Ryland, American tennis player (first African-American to play tennis professionally) and coach (Harold Solomon, Arthur Ashe, Venus & Serena Williams), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2020)
John Howard Griffin, American photographer, journalist and author (Black Like Me), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1980)
José López Portillo, President of Mexico (1976-82), born in Mexico City (d. 2004)
Raymond Urgel Lemieux, Canadian scientist, born in Lac La Biche, Alberta (d. 2000)
Frances Rafferty, American WWII pin-up girl, dancer and actress (Bud Abbott and Lou Costello in Hollywood), born in Sioux City, Iowa (d. 2004)
Rinus Terlouw, Dutch soccer defender (34 caps; Sparta 248 games), born in Capelle aan den IJssel, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Åke Hermanson, Swedish composer, born in Mollösund, Sweden (d. 1996)
Henryk Czyz, Polish composer and musician, born in Grudziądz (d. 2003)
Ron Flockhart, Scottish auto racer (24 Hours of Le Mans 1956, 57), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1962)
(Eli) "Lucky" Thompson, American jazz tenor and soprano saxophonist (Stan Kenton - Cuban Fire!; Milt Jackson; Miles Davis - Walkin'), born in Columbia, South Carolina (d. 2005)
Idries Shah, Indian author and Sufi teacher, born in Simla, British India (d. 1996)
Bebe Barron (née Charlotte Wind), American composer and electronic music pioneer (with her husband Louis - Forbidden Planet), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2008)
Faith Domergue, American actress and early "scream queen" (Vendetta, House of 7 Corpses), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1999)
Richard Jacobs, American real estate developer, philanthropist, and sports team owner (Cleveland Indians, 1986-99), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2009)
Rex Cowan, British solicitor and shipwreck explorer who rediscovered the Hollandia, born in Golders Green, England (d. 2025)
Tom Graveney, England cricket batsman (79 Tests; top score 258; 732 first class games), born in Riding Mill, Northumberland (d. 2015)
Dagmar Rom, Austrian alpine ski racer (World C'ship gold slalom & giant slalom 1950; Olympic silver giant slalom 1952), born in Innsbruck, Austria (d. 2022)
John Cuneo, Australian sailor (Olympic gold dragon class 1972), born in Bulimba, Queensland, Australia (d. 2020)
Sergiu Comissiona, Romanian-Israeli-American conductor (Haifa Symphony, 1959-64; Gothenburg Symphony, 1966-77; Philharmonic; Baltimore Symphony, 1969-84), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 2005)
James K. Randall, American composer, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 2014)
Pauline Yates, British actress (The Fall and Rise of Reginald Perrin), born in St Helens, Merseyside (d. 2015)
Ramon Bieri, American actor (Badlands, The Andromeda Strain), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2001)
Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, 5th Emir of Kuwait (2006-20), Commander of the Kuwait Military Forces, born in Kuwait City, Kuwait (d. 2020)
Philip Sunshine, American physician who helped revolutionize neonatal care for premature babies, born in Denver, Colorado (d. 2025)
Vilmos Zsigmond, Hungarian-American cinematographer (Close Encounters of the Third Kind), born in Szeged, Hungary (d. 2016)
Courtney Alexandre Henriques Laws, British community activist, born in Jamaica (d. 1996)
Don Elbaum, American boxing promoter (matchmaker for over 10,000 fights, promoted over 1,000 cards), born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 2025)
Ivo Petric, Slovenian composer, born in Ljubljana, Slovenia (d. 2018)
Robert Freeman Smith, American politician (Rep-R-Oregon), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 2020)
Ralph Robins, British former CEO of Rolls-Royce, born in England
